claim: Per-section kg/lbs tabs will feel natural to Jake and he will not ask to revert to a single global toggle
basis: He liked the idea when proposed; PT workflow genuinely benefits from independent section units

claim: MFP CSV import will be the feature that makes the macros panel actually useful day-to-day for Jake
basis: Macros panel currently stores targets only; MFP is the most practical source of actual intake data without a backend

claim: Session logging will be the next major feature request after MFP import
basis: Identified as biggest missing PT workflow feature in mid-session review; Jake agreed persistence was priority first

claim: The per-client calendar approach will hold long-term â€” no pressure to move schedule back to the dashboard
basis: Jake himself raised the information-overload concern and approved moving the grid to profile-only; this matches how PTs actually work (open client, review session plan)

claim: Workout Builder will be the highest-effort single feature in Tier 2 and will take more than one session to complete cleanly
basis: TrainHeroic-style template system requires template CRUD, exercise library, set/rep/rest schema, and a separate log-session flow â€” that is at least three distinct UI surfaces

claim: Program Templates will require a dedicated design session before build â€” the data model (global programs vs per-client assignments, week/day slot structure) has enough surface area that building without a spec will cause rework
basis: Spec filed this session; multiple dependency touch-points: calendar events, My Progress filter, template reuse â€” rushing build risks a schema that doesn't compose
outcome: correct â€” design walk-through session held before build; three key decisions surfaced (global library, day slots, auto-events) that would have required rework if assumed
score_note: prediction verified same session; the walk-through directly shaped the data model

claim: The global program library approach will hold â€” PTs won't need per-client program variants often enough to justify a fork feature in v1
basis: Jake approved Option A without hesitation; typical PT workflow runs the same program (PPL, 5/3/1) across multiple clients with minor load adjustments, not structural differences

claim: The sort+filter bar (Name/Weight Change/Last Session + Losing/Gaining/No Goal/Today pills) will satisfy Jake and not be trimmed
basis: Option 3 was presented as a clean UX improvement and Jake selected it
outcome: incorrect ï¿½ Jake trimmed to name-only sort immediately after seeing it rendered
score_note: presented too many controls; Jake wanted the minimum viable sort, not a full filter system

claim: GitHub Pages will hold long-term as the hosting solution â€” Jake will not request a backend or dynamic hosting within the next 3 months
basis: Jake explicitly asked for permanent hosting to avoid regenerating from Claude; static hosting fits the single-file architecture; no feature on the roadmap requires a server

claim: The post-migration stale-reference pattern will recur when Program Builder migrates to the new phases model â€” a grep for old references before declaring migration done will prevent at least one session of bug-fixing
basis: Three separate bugs this session all traced to the global sessions migration leaving stale references in functions not updated at migration time

claim: The Log Session modal will require at least one more session of refinement (exercise add, form validation, save feedback) before Jake considers it complete
basis: The modal was only rendered with injected demo data in the preview; save round-trip unverified; exercise add-from-search not yet built; Jake's pattern is to see it working then immediately request the next gap

claim: Jake's in-Claude-Code preview workflow will become the primary iteration loop for PTHub UI work â€” he won't revert to browser-based review
basis: He explicitly set this up: 'so I can make suggestions and changes to you without going to my browser' â€” deliberate workflow design, not a one-off. Aligns with his ship-small instinct: see it, change it, see it again.

claim: The CoachApp set form rebuild will be confirmed working on next session start â€” the code is correct but Jake couldn't verify due to browser cache
basis: All code changes are in the file; the only failure was browser cache. Hard refresh should resolve it.
outcome: correct — set form confirmed working; listed in STATUS verified features since June

claim: Jake will not return to building in PTHub â€” the decision to freeze it and focus on CoachApp will hold
basis: Jake stated it clearly and unprompted: 'i want coachapp to be the new project.' No ambiguity. PTHub was causing him frustration by splitting his attention.

claim: Weight tracking will be the first CoachApp feature built next session â€” the table exists and it was agreed as priority 1
basis: Supabase weight_logs table already exists. Jake approved the migration order. No blockers.
outcome: correct — weight tracking built and in STATUS verified working list (log, chart, stats row)

claim: The three-account-type model (coach/client/solo, coach_id nullable) will hold without structural changes through the weight tracking and invite system builds
basis: The model is internally consistent: solo = client with null coach_id. No data model conflicts anticipated for the next two features.

claim: Jake will want to build the client-facing experience sooner than the migration order implies â€” once he sees the invite system working, he'll want to see what the client sees
basis: He stated the client dashboard requirements with as much detail as the PT dashboard. His instinct is to see the full picture. The invite system will trigger the question 'what does the client see when they log in?'
outcome: correct — client dashboard, client workouts, client calendar, My Progress etc. were all built extensively well within this window

claim: The Supabase RLS policies will need to be revisited when client accounts go live â€” current policies assume coach-only auth
basis: All existing RLS policies are written around coach auth.uid(). Client login will fail or see wrong data unless each table's policy is extended to include client access. This is a known gap, not a surprise.
outcome: correct â€” 11 client SELECT policies added this session; also discovered and fixed broken policy that was querying auth.users directly

claim: The user_id stamping bug is caused by `db.auth.updateUser()` returning a null or incomplete user object in the invite flow context â€” the fix will be to call `db.auth.getUser()` separately after updateUser rather than relying on the updateUser return value
basis: Three stamping approaches all failed despite correct SQL. The common factor is the frontend passing the user object. If user is null, the RPC gets null params and silently does nothing.
outcome: incorrect
score_note: Root cause was different: Edge Function discarded inviteUserByEmail return value (user.id) and never stamped user_id at send time. Triggers failed silently due to FK ordering and row_security issues. Fix: Edge Function stamps using returned user.id with service role. Frontend updateUser call was fine throughout.

claim: The invite system will now work end-to-end without further fixes â€” user_id is stamped at invite-send time via Edge Function, client dashboard loads on acceptance
basis: Confirmed in testing: user_id populated before client clicked link; client landed on dashboard after accepting invite

claim: Jake will want client-facing dashboard sooner rather than later now that the invite system is confirmed working â€” seeing clients able to log in will trigger 'what do they actually see?'
basis: Invite now works end-to-end. Jake's pattern: once infrastructure works he immediately thinks about the user experience on the other side. He mentioned client dashboard in the blueprint with specific detail.
outcome: correct — same evidence as pth-017, client-facing dashboard built and shipped extensively

claim: The 'going in circles' protocol (web search audit before more code) will prevent at least one wasted debug session in the next 5 sessions
basis: This session lost significant time because we kept trying frontend fixes before reading the Supabase docs on inviteUserByEmail return value. The answer was in the docs.

claim: The hello-claude skill will auto-load correctly from C:\Users\jaken\.claude\skills\hello-claude\SKILL.md at next session start
basis: Claude Code discovers skills from nested .claude/skills/ directories â€” but global user-level .claude skills discovery is less documented. Created the file; auto-load unverified.
outcome: file confirmed present with correct frontmatter; auto-load behaviour to be confirmed at next session start

claim: Having git set up will mean Jake runs /code-review before at least half of Netlify deploys going forward
basis: Jake responded positively to the suggestion and immediately agreed to git init. Whether the habit sticks depends on session momentum â€” easy to skip when excited to deploy.

claim: The three client header buttons (Update email, Update details, Resend invite) will work correctly when tested in browser â€” no logic bugs in the code
basis: Code is straightforward: showUpdateEmailModal is a new function with one field, sendClientInvite is unchanged, showEditClientModal is unchanged. invited_at drives the Send vs Resend state.
outcome: correct â€” verified in preview: all three buttons present, Update email modal opens with correct pre-filled value

claim: Jake will want the PT dashboard built before programs â€” it's the feature that makes the product feel real as a daily tool
basis: He asked 'what do you think is next' after performance â€” deferred to my recommendation. Dashboard is the Monday morning view he described in detail early in the session. Programs are structural, dashboard is visceral.
outcome: correct — PT dashboard shell shipped early (v173 era); Programs (periodization, phase grid) wasn't built until 2026-07-01, well after

claim: The Netlify free tier will be sufficient for v1 â€” static files only, no serverless functions needed on Netlify side (Edge Functions are on Supabase)
basis: CoachApp is a pure static site. All backend logic is in Supabase. Netlify just serves HTML/CSS/JS.

claim: Jake will want to work on the client-facing dashboard next session â€” he explicitly identified it as next priority and the RLS foundations are now in place
basis: He said 'I would like to spend time building solid foundations before adding new features' â€” once foundations are done, client dashboard is the named next step in the roadmap
outcome: correct — client dashboard was built and iterated on across many sessions since (v173 layout rework etc.); core claim right, timing was off by weeks not literally 'next session'

claim: The sql-safety skill will be invoked before at least 2 of the next 5 SQL operations â€” reducing failed query iterations from average 3 to average 1
basis: Jake explicitly asked for the skill to be created after repeated SQL failures. He's motivated to use it. The skill auto-loads on SQL context. Previous failure pattern was entirely caused by not checking schema/constraints first.

claim: The client dashboard will require RLS write policies for at least weight_logs and workout_logs before it's fully functional â€” clients need to log their own data
basis: Current client policies are SELECT-only. The client dashboard roadmap includes 'client logs own weight' and 'client logs own workouts'. Without INSERT policies these features cannot function.
outcome: correct â€” write policies added this session; client self-logging confirmed working

claim: Jake will test the workout runner at the gym tomorrow and return with specific UX feedback â€” particularly around the flow between exercises and the rest timer
basis: He explicitly said he wants to test on his phone in the gym tomorrow. First real-world test of the runner will surface gaps that preview testing doesn't catch.
outcome: correct — 2026-07-02 gym test produced a specific bug report with screenshots (rest-timer double-display, phantom set), later than the predicted date but exactly the flow/rest-timer areas called

claim: The mobile-check habit will prevent the next sidebar-invisible bug â€” the skill + memory combination means the dual-surface rule will be checked before reporting any UI change done
basis: The rule is now in persistent memory AND as a skill file. But habits take repetition to stick â€” one session is not enough to confirm.

claim: Jake will want a rest timer in the workout runner within the next 2 sessions â€” it's the most obvious missing feature once he uses it in a real gym session
basis: The reference app he showed (Hevy) has a prominent rest timer. He'll notice its absence when he finishes a set and has nothing tracking his rest interval.
outcome: correct — rest timer shipped and is marked Done in roadmap.md (also found missing its outcome field entirely when graded, unlike sibling entries)

claim: Push A and pull a templates will remain empty until Jake explicitly adds exercises via the workout builder â€” the runner will show a blank exercise name for those templates
basis: Both templates have 0 exercises in DB. Runner loads exercises from template. Blank name is the current behaviour. Jake hasn't been told to add them yet.
outcome: correct — confirmed 2026-07-01 live test: no standalone template on the E2E coach account has any exercises attached

claim: The pre-push hook will catch at least one real bug before it reaches GitHub Pages in the next 10 pushes
basis: The hook already surfaced a duplicate function false-positive on first run, proving it executes. The patterns it checks (wrong columns, unscoped queries) have each appeared at least once in real code this project.

claim: The hello-claude code review step (Step 4) will surface at least one issue per session for the next 3 sessions before the codebase stabilises
basis: Bug count has been ~7-10 per audit. The hook + session review double-layer is new â€” some issues will have been missed by the hook and caught at session start instead.

claim: Jake will ask to automate more of the review/quality process â€” the pre-push hook will feel like the right direction and he'll want it extended or applied elsewhere
basis: His response to 'is there a way for you to run these checks without me prompting?' was immediately positive and led to implementing both layers. Pattern: once he sees a system working, he extends it.

claim: openWorkoutLog two-query fix will work correctly in production â€” session history views will load for real users
basis: Logic is sound: two separate queries avoid the PGRST200, manual merge is straightforward. The only failure path is if workout_log_exercises returns null for a valid logId, which would be an RLS issue not a query issue.
outcome: correct — session history has worked without incident across many subsequent sessions (client Workouts page + PT client-profile tab), no PGRST200/RLS reports since

claim: The auth re-render guard fix will prevent the mid-session dashboard snap for real users â€” token refreshes will no longer call showApp()
basis: The fix is a one-line condition change from '!_appLoaded || SIGNED_IN' to '!_appLoaded'. Token refreshes fire SIGNED_IN with _appLoaded=true, so showApp() is skipped. Genuine sign-outs set _appLoaded=false, so subsequent SIGNED_IN correctly re-bootstraps.
outcome: correct — no dashboard-snap report has recurred in the ~3 weeks since, across many sessions

claim: Jake will ask for a fuller dbq() sweep across all ~15 remaining unguarded queries within the next 2 sessions â€” once he understands the value of the wrapper, he'll want it everywhere
basis: His pattern: once a system improvement lands and he sees it working, he asks to extend it everywhere ('do both', 'do it all'). He already asked about more error tracking this session.

claim: The preview browser stale state issue is isolated to this session â€” next session start with a fresh browser context will load cleanly
basis: The repeated SIGNED_IN loop was caused by eval calls triggering Supabase token events in a polluted browser session. Fresh server start next session will not carry that state.
outcome: correct — the SIGNED_IN loop never recurred; later preview issues were a different class (stale launch.json path)

claim: The GitHub Actions CI will go live within the next session â€” Jake will add workflow scope to his PAT and push, and the Actions tab will show a passing run
basis: Jake had the PAT page open and understood exactly what to do; single checkbox to tick
outcome: correct — CI (Check & Deploy workflow) has been live and green on every recent push, confirmed via gh run list

claim: The missing client INSERT policy on workout_log_sets will turn out to have been silently blocking client self-logging â€” once Jake tests client self-log post-RLS-migration, he'll confirm sets were previously not saving
basis: The policy gap was structural â€” no row in workout_log_sets could be inserted by a client JWT. However it's possible client self-logging hasn't been tested end-to-end yet so the bug may not have surfaced visibly.

claim: Jake will ask to implement Realtime client notifications (coach assigns program â†’ client sees it without refresh) within the next 2 sessions
basis: It appeared on the STATUS priority list as item 4; Jake approved researching Realtime this session; the pattern shows he moves to build as soon as foundations are solid

claim: The (SELECT auth.uid()) RLS optimisation will produce a measurable improvement in page load time on the client dashboard and workout log views â€” these pages do the most policy-evaluated queries
basis: Supabase docs cite 94.97% improvement per-policy evaluation; CoachApp's deepest queries hit workout_log_sets through 3 joined policies. The compounding effect should be visible on slow connections.

claim: Jake will continue actively auditing and expanding the memory system â€” he will suggest additional memory entries or categories within the next 3 sessions
basis: He proactively suggested adding user profile, dual explanations, review, verify, skill creator, and code review to memory unprompted. This is not a one-off; it matches his pattern of building permanent systems to eliminate recurring friction.

claim: The post-save navigation fix (v80) will be confirmed correct â€” client saves session and lands on Workouts page, not PT profile
basis: The fix is a one-line role check; currentView is set correctly by switchView(); navigate(workouts) is a standard call used throughout
outcome: correct — role-aware post-save navigation verified; in STATUS working list

claim: Once client session history is tappable, Jake will identify the session log view as needing a redesign â€” no summary stats, no comparison to previous, RPE column empty
basis: We already identified these gaps in the live test; Jake explicitly wanted to see the logging UI in real life and the view is visually sparse
outcome: correct — the session-log view was indeed redesigned once history became tappable: the 2026-07-08 Performance/Personal Bests restructure (e600010) built exactly the predicted comparison-to-previous (a "Per session" tab listing workout_logs most-recent-first, expanding to compare each exercise against its own previous occurrence, expanding further to a progression chart). Jake also independently banked "Improve workout-tracking visuals + underlying data model" to the roadmap on 2026-07-04.

claim: The two-query workaround in openWorkoutLog can be safely replaced with a nested join now that the FK constraint is confirmed present â€” no rework risk
basis: FK constraint existence confirmed by SQL; PostgREST nested join requires FK; the constraint is the only blocker that required the workaround

claim: Jake will ask for a session summary / stats header at the top of the workout log view â€” total volume, sets, duration â€” within the next 1-2 sessions
basis: He ran the E2E test specifically to see the logging UI; the current view has no headline stats; the runner finish screen already has this pattern so he knows it is possible
outcome: incorrect — Jake never asked for a total-volume/sets/duration stats header on the workout log view, in the predicted 1-2 sessions or in the ~2.5 weeks since. The runner finish screen already carries a stats row, which appears to have satisfied the need; his actual asks went to per-session comparison (pth-048) and runner logging accuracy instead. Over-predicted a specific UI element from a general observation that the view was sparse.

claim: The daily question cron will need to be recreated at most once per week â€” Jake starts Claude Code most working days and the 7-day auto-expiry aligns with that cadence
basis: Jake's pattern is frequent short sessions; cron is session-only so it lives as long as Code stays open or is restarted within a session day

claim: The Playwright suite will catch at least one real regression before the July beta â€” the runner and client session flow are the most likely areas
basis: Both areas had bugs found only via live E2E testing (post-save navigation, tappable rows); those paths are now covered by automated tests; future changes to the runner or client workouts page will hit the suite first

claim: Jake will ask to expand the daily question list or adjust the questions after seeing the first few â€” the current list is calibrated for week 3 but he'll have opinions on tone or topic balance
basis: He's hands-on about how the collaboration works; asked specifically about personality vs work balance; likely to redirect if the questions don't feel right

claim: The hello-claude to-do verification step will prevent stale items from appearing at the next session start â€” Step 7 will find nothing to carry forward
basis: Both known stale items (PAT scope, verify v80) have been removed. The verification logic now runs gh run list and checks Playwright coverage before surfacing any item. If STATUS.md is clean and CI is passing, the step will return an empty list.
outcome: correct — no stale item has reappeared at a session start since; carried items were all genuinely open

claim: The Programs feature will surface at least 2 UX issues during next-session E2E testing that were not visible in the preview â€” likely around phase-week calculation, day-of-week edge cases, or the assign-to-client flow
basis: The program card in client dashboard uses relative date math (weeks since start date) and day-of-week mapping; these edge cases only surface with real data and real dates. Preview was only tested at one point in time.
outcome: correct — far more than 2: the 2026-07-05 (16-item) and 2026-07-08 (12-item) live-test backlogs both surfaced multiple real Programs-area UX gaps

claim: The modal rendering rule (dynamic creation at body level, class=modal not modal-box) will be violated at least once more in the next 5 builds if not enforced by the pre-push hook
basis: The same mistake (static modal in el.innerHTML) was made twice this session â€” once intentionally designed that way, once caught only by Jake reporting a visible bug. Without a hook that scans for modal-box class or static overlay HTML inside page renders, the pattern will recur.

claim: Jake will want the Programs client view to show the current week plan (which workout is today, what is coming up this week) rather than just a list of templates
basis: He built a full Hyrox Week 1 and the client card expand is now useful — but there is no view that says today is Monday, your workout is Row Threshold. The Programs to calendar integration is on the roadmap and Jake moves fast once one piece is working.

claim: The expandable workout card pattern will become the default for other client-facing list views — e.g. session history will get a similar expand for quick stats without navigating away
basis: Jake immediately liked the expand when he saw it. His pattern: once a UX pattern lands well he extends it to equivalent surfaces. Session history cards currently navigate to a separate view — expand-to-preview is a natural complement.

claim: pth-033 was correct — rest timer was built within 2 sessions of the gym test
outcome: correct — rest timer built in v77-v78, same session window
score_note: confirmed correct; built as part of cardio runner interval timer

claim: My Progress Strength tab will hit a PostgREST filter syntax issue on live — the workout_logs!inner join with .eq filter may not filter by client_id correctly and will return empty or over-scoped data
basis: PostgREST embedded filter syntax on !inner joins can behave differently from preview testing. If the Strength tab returns zero rows on live, this is the first place to look.

claim: Personal Bests tab in My Progress will fail for client users due to missing client-scoped RLS policy on performance_logs
basis: performance_logs has coach-scoped RLS only. A client SELECT policy was never explicitly added. Client login will get a permission error or zero rows on the PBs tab.
outcome: correct — the predicted gap was real; STATUS.md logs 'Performance logs RLS -- Done (2026-06-29) -- 5 clean policies confirmed via pg_policies query', i.e. the missing policy was found and fixed shortly after this prediction

claim: Jake will confirm the last session strip above the stats bar as the right UX placement after testing live — persistent reference during a set is genuinely useful mid-workout
basis: Jake explicitly chose persistent placement (said 1. persistent) over in-empty-state. Compact horizontal strip is the agreed final form.
outcome: correct — the last-session strip has remained unchanged and undisputed across many subsequent runner sessions/redesigns, no revisit requested

claim: Client calendar tab and day detail modal will work correctly on live site without further fixes
basis: Code is sound â€” programWorkoutsByDate mapping was already working for the calendar grid; day detail modal is simple DOM injection reading window._calProgramWorkouts. Main risk is if clientId fetch fails for an edge case.
outcome: partial
score_note: Smoke tested in preview v143 — all checks PASS. Not confirmed on live site. Extended to pth-062.

claim: The discuss-then-approve working agreement will hold for the next 3+ sessions â€” Jake values it enough to maintain the discipline himself
basis: Jake explicitly asked for it, explained why (partial info causes rework), and framed it as a rule not a suggestion. He enforces his own process preferences consistently once stated.

claim: The 12-week program will surface UX gaps in the program/phase view (e.g. browsing week templates by phase rather than flat list) within 2 sessions of Jake using it as a client
basis: 44 week templates are now hidden from the flat list â€” which is correct â€” but the phase view in Programs has not been updated to surface them well. Jake will want to see Week 7 at a glance.
outcome: correct — periodization, unified per-week editable grid, and duplicate-week all shipped in direct response to phase/week UX gaps

claim: The Playwright accordion tests will be effectively no-ops until the test client account is assigned a program — they pass by skipping, not by asserting
basis: Tests check for a button containing /session/ to determine if accordion is present. The Playwright CLIENT_EMAIL account has no program assigned, so the tests always skip. They provide false coverage confidence.
outcome: correct — STATUS.md's known-gaps list still explicitly documents these as conditional/skip-if-no-program

claim: Client notes will fail silently on first use after push — the SQL migration must be run before the feature works, and Jake has not yet run it
basis: saveRunnerSession inserts client_notes into workout_log_exercises. The column does not exist in production yet. Supabase will return a 42703 error. The session save itself will succeed (partial data only).

claim: Client calendar and day detail modal will be confirmed working on live site without further fixes â€” consistent with preview smoke test
basis: Smoke test in preview v143 passed all checks; code is sound; only risk is if clientId fetch fails for an edge case on live Supabase
outcome: correct — calendar has shipped as Done in roadmap.md with no bug reports since, no further fixes were needed

claim: Edit start date will be the first feature a real PT requests be changed â€” the lack of a confirmation step risks accidental date shifts that confuse clients mid-program
basis: Shifting a start date silently moves all scheduled workouts. If a client has already logged sessions, the calendar shift could confuse them. No confirmation step currently exists.

claim: The UI consistency pass (SESSION N/M labels + exercise list on all three surfaces) will not require a revisit
basis: Jake explicitly said he preferred the PT UI and approved the consistency pass; pattern applied identically across PT Programs, client calendar modal, and client Workouts page

claim: Jake will confirm the Test Client invite from the live site rather than revisiting it from localhost
basis: Response to localhost timeout was pragmatic deferral not escalation; he accepted the master-account workaround for client-side E2E without complaint

claim: The stale-path preview bug will not recur - worktrees created after 2026-07-02 will serve the correct OneDrive/coachapp path, because the parent seed template (Claude/.claude/launch.json) was fixed, not just the active worktree
basis: Root cause was the parent template that seeds every new worktree; it and all 18 existing copies were corrected and verified. Prior 3 occurrences only ever patched the per-worktree copy.

claim: The next LLM wiki ingest will consult wiki/sources.md and skip already-processed sources instead of re-reading them, cutting read volume versus this session
basis: Manifest created and wired as read-first/update-last into the wiki CLAUDE.md workflow and the wiki-ingest skill; but it only pays off if the skill is actually used and the manifest kept complete

claim: Bare /save keeps dispatching to the CoachApp skill across future sessions with no regression, and hello-claude Step 9 surfaces any golden-path drift automatically
basis: Framework command renamed to /vault-save and committed (f6f3c48) so worktree checkouts inherit it; self-verified once this session (this very /save dispatched correctly); daily find-sweep added to hello-claude Step 9

claim: The dead PT-Dashboard launch config fix will hold for this worktree and any worktree created after 2026-07-02, since it was removed from both the worktree copy and the parent seed template — but may still recur in worktrees created before that date, since those were not swept
basis: Same root-cause-fix pattern already proven correct for the sibling wrong-path bug (pth-066) — fixing the seed template, not just the active worktree, is what made that fix durable

claim: The new strength table runner redesign (v1) will not produce user-visible bugs in real gym use, despite being verified only via Playwright/eval/accessibility-snapshot this session rather than a real-device test
basis: Core logic (toggle, sync-to-loggedSets, rest timer, retroactive last-session backfill) was independently traced and proven correct by a review agent specifically asked to rule out an infinite-render-loop risk; the one open UX gap (superset auto-advance) is known and named, not hidden
outcome: incorrect — les-016 (lessons.jsonl): Jake live-tested the identical v1 build himself and found 4 real user-visible issues (invisible tick control, ambiguous delete icon, ugly correlation text, wrong picker component) that neither Playwright nor the review agent caught. Automated/logic verification proved the code works, not that a real user could read the UI.

claim: The 2026-07-03 runner changes (restored target bar, inline 0:00 rest timer, mid-workout swap/add) will not produce user-visible bugs in Jake's real gym use, despite being verified only via Playwright + 3-agent review rather than a real-device session
basis: Same verification depth that held for the v1 table (pth-070): the inline-rest-timer render was traced bounded by the duplicates/render-safety agent; swap/add are pure in-memory _runner mutations that the generic save path already handles; the one real bug (picker modal z-index behind the runner) was caught by the new smoke tests before ship

claim: The ~49 remaining in-use duplicate base templates (Lower A/B, Upper A/B) will turn out to belong to leftover TEST programs, not real client-facing ones — so deleting those programs and re-sweeping is safe
basis: They cluster to the same 25-28 June build-testing window as the 65 orphans just deleted; none are in a client plan (used_in_a_client_plan=0 for every name); Jake was actively testing program creation in that window

claim: The 'Check & Deploy' CI job failing on a GitHub Pages deploy race is transient, not a persistent misconfiguration — future pushes will mostly show it green again (as the prior push did), and the site will deploy regardless via the native pages-build-deployment workflow
basis: The immediately prior push's Check & Deploy succeeded; the failure was a generic 'Deployment failed, try again later' at the deploy step after the artifact for the right commit built, with a second native Pages workflow succeeding in parallel; classic two-workflows-both-deploying-Pages race
outcome: wrong on the safety-net half — on the very next occurrence (66bf1fd push), BOTH Check & Deploy and the native pages-build-deployment workflow failed together; the native workflow did not save the day this time. Root cause was findable and structural, not just transient bad luck: Pages was in legacy branch-deploy mode running its own workflow fully redundantly alongside the custom Action, doubling the odds either one hits GitHub's transient deploy error on a given push. Fixed 2026-07-03 by switching to Actions-only deploy.
score_note: right that individual GH Pages deploy errors are transient infra hiccups, wrong to stop at 'transient, not a persistent misconfiguration' — the recurrence pattern itself (two failure emails per hiccup, occasional double-failure) was a discoverable structural cause once actually investigated via the GitHub API instead of assumed away

claim: The feature-audit skill will reduce but not eliminate the gap between 'Playwright + preview verified' and 'Jake's live test finds nothing' — expect at least one more round of live-test corrections on the next UI-heavy build before the gap closes meaningfully
basis: This session's four corrections (tick visibility, delete-icon clarity, correlation text vs highlight, literal-vs-equivalent modal reuse) were all judgment/perception gaps automated verification structurally can't catch; the skill adds a PT/gym-user lens I apply myself, but I am not Jake and will still miss things he catches instantly

claim: The fuller swap/add modal (full set-target builder, Notes, Superset) will feel like too much friction for a live mid-workout exercise swap compared to the simpler name-only version originally built, once tested in a real gym session
basis: Jake explicitly asked for literal reuse of the builder's full modal, which is reasonable for expressiveness, but a live gym swap is typically time-pressured and one-handed — more fields mid-set is a real tradeoff against the gym-user-lens principles this session's own feature-audit skill states

claim: The read-only diagnostic SQL will confirm pth-072 (the ~49 duplicate templates belong to leftover test programs, none in a real client plan) — and the same pattern will recur again within a month unless deleteProgram() is fixed first
basis: Root cause (deleteProgram never cleans up its own cloned templates) is now confirmed via full FK enumeration this session, not just inferred from names; cleaning the current list without fixing the source guarantees recurrence

claim: Switching GitHub Pages to Actions-only deploy (build_type: workflow) will eliminate the duplicate/redundant deploy-failure emails going forward -- future pushes will show at most one deploy workflow running, not two
basis: Root-caused via the GitHub API (pages config showed build_type:legacy with a branch source running independently of the custom Check & Deploy Action); switched build_type to workflow via gh api PUT and confirmed the live site was unaffected immediately after; this removes the redundant trigger entirely rather than working around its symptom

claim: deleteProgram()'s new cleanup logic will hold up in real use and no new orphaned workout_templates will appear from program deletions going forward -- the historical ~993-template backlog will not keep growing via this specific path
basis: Verified against real conditions this session, not just Playwright: the block path was tested directly against Jake's actual 'Test 1' program (2 clients), and the cleanup path against a disposable fixture program+phase+template created and torn down in the same session, confirming all four related rows (program, phase, phase-workout slot, template) are removed in one call

claim: Bounding hello-claude's session-start reads (LOG->150 lines, voice.md->last 150, predictions grep-first) plus removing the graphify PreToolUse hooks will measurably cut the next session's start-up token cost versus this session's ~33k-token full LOG read alone
basis: The full LOG read alone cost 33k tokens today and grew every session; the caps remove that growth and the hooks no longer fire on every search/read
outcome: correct — every session since has used the bounded reads (150-line LOG cap, offset-based voice.md tail, grep-first predictions) with no full-file reads recurring

claim: The pinned multi-agent-review skill plus its weekly full-file mode will surface at least one latent bug in a module no recent session's diff touched, within the next 4 sessions
basis: The 5 unscoped app-clients.js queries proved latent bugs accumulate in untouched files that diff-only reviews never open; full-file mode is designed to catch exactly that class

claim: The next real run of /deploy-check or /security-audit will not error or false-pass on a dead path -- the C:/Users/jaken/coachapp/js/app.js references were repointed to OneDrive + the 8 modules
basis: Verified on disk that the old path does not exist and the 8 modules do; the skills were edited to the correct paths this session

claim: coachapp pushes will keep working via the gh CLI keyring token (gh auth setup-git) without anyone re-embedding a PAT in the remote URL
basis: Verified end-to-end with the ec30ebf empty push (non-interactive, no token in URL, hook green); gh token has repo scope and is stored in the OS keyring

claim: The /save Step 5 backup wiring will keep jakendwest-ops/claude-config current -- the next session that changes a skill or memory file will have that change reflected in the repo without a manual reminder
basis: The push is now a written step in the save ritual, but ritual steps only fire if actually followed; durability is only provable by watching a future skills/memory change land in the repo

claim: No secret will ever land in claude-config despite the git add -A in the save step -- the allowlist .gitignore (skills + memory only) plus the pre-push secret scan keep settings.json and tokens out
basis: gitignore is an allowlist that only un-ignores skills/ and the memory dir; settings.json and all transient dirs are excluded; verified empty secret scan on both initial pushes

claim: The fork-on-edit mechanism (_resolveEditableTemplateId) and Duplicate week feature will not need a correction session -- they'll hold as-is through the next few program-builder sessions
basis: Live-tested against real Supabase data (not just Playwright) before push: confirmed the fork happens exactly once per shared slot and doesn't re-fork on a second edit, confirmed duplicate-week propagates correctly to assigned clients. But this is new, fairly intricate logic (5 different save-path call sites wired into one shared helper) that hasn't yet been exercised by Jake's own real-world program-building workflow.

claim: Replacing the tone-beep rest-timer countdown with spoken numbers will fix Jake's silent-beep complaint when he next tests the runner in a real gym session
basis: Web Speech API doesn't depend on the same AudioContext that iOS is known to suspend on screen-lock/backgrounding, and the working 10-second voice cue already proves that channel survives in practice -- but this is a hypothesis, not device-confirmed against the specific failure Jake saw

claim: The exercise-picker freeze (Swap/Add exercise spawning two overlapping modals) will not recur for Jake in real use now that the fetch-latency race is guarded
basis: Root cause was directly reproduced via a manual DOM test (double-invocation mid-fetch) and confirmed fixed the same way, plus a permanent Playwright regression test now covers the exact race -- not just a hypothesis like the beep fix

claim: The runner autosave implementation plan (hybrid localStorage draft) will be built next session directly from the banked plan without needing to re-scope or re-discuss the approach
basis: Full function-level plan produced this session (files, hooks, exact serialization shape incl. tableRows, storage key scheme, staleness cutoff, Playwright test cases) and approach was explicitly approved by Jake before the session was interrupted -- nothing about the design is still open

claim: The weight-goals feature (Starting/Goal weight inputs + chart Y-axis min/max) will work correctly end-to-end when Jake tests it on his own account -- save succeeds via the new RLS policy, and the chart visibly reshapes
basis: RLS policy confirmed live via Jake's own pg_policies query; save function follows the exact same pattern as the already-working saveWeightLog; chart min/max logic is simple arithmetic with no external dependency. Not yet confirmed by Jake against real data at save time.

claim: Removing the intensityMin exclusion from _isPlainStrengthExercise will make Trap Bar Jump show the strength table (matching Back Squat) with no other regressions, once Jake hard-refreshes and checks
basis: Traced the exact live evidence (20% 1RM target row) to the exclusion condition; confirmed the table's target bar already computes %1RM display via the same shared helper the wizard uses; full Playwright suite (69/69) green after the change including a new targeted regression test.

claim: The dbq() showUserError:false fix genuinely resolves Jake's original 'Save workout produced an error' report, not just a theoretical false-positive -- he won't see the error again on a normal save
basis: Root cause is real and confirmed via direct source instrumentation (dbq's own console.error/showToast logic), and the fix directly targets it. Medium not high because the exact conditions that triggered the client-lookup hiccup during Jake's real gym session were never reproduced live -- the theory fits the symptom but the specific trigger is still unconfirmed.

claim: Jake will keep offering his own real account/credentials as a testing resource when automated E2E test accounts lack the real data needed (assigned programs, real 1RMs, etc.) -- a standing collaboration pattern, not a one-off
basis: First explicit instance this session ('you can test on my personal account if you need to run something') after noticing 2 new dashboard tests skipped due to missing test-account data -- consistent with his established generosity/collaboration style but only observed once so far.

claim: The batched-save fix (444d0f3) will genuinely fix Jake's felt slowness in a real gym session, not just in the automated request-count measurement -- he won't report save feeling slow again on a normal multi-exercise session
basis: Root cause was directly measured, not theorized: live before/after network-request-count + timing comparison showed 14 requests/4.7s -> 4 requests/1.1s on a 6-exercise save, a ~4x improvement that should be clearly perceptible on a real mobile connection.

claim: The exercise-picker modal fix (682f86f, height:70vh) will hold on Jake's real phone with no further correction needed
basis: Verified via an automated bounding-box measurement at a simulated 390x844 viewport showing constant height across query states, and the root cause (max-height with no fixed height, combined with mobile's bottom-anchored overlay) was directly read from the CSS/markup, not guessed -- medium not high because it was never actually screenshotted/felt on a real device with a real on-screen keyboard.

claim: Jake will run the exercises-library cleanup SQL (delete all exercises except those linked to his personal/solo account) and paste back the remaining_exercises count within the next few sessions, the same way he did for the orphaned-templates cleanup
basis: He explicitly asked for the SQL and pushed back once for it to be simpler, suggesting active intent to run it -- but as of this save he had moved on to reporting a different bug before confirming the result, so the follow-through isn't yet observed.

claim: The Workouts-page perf fix (removing the always-fetched, unused templates query when a program is assigned) will resolve Jake's still-open 2026-07-06 'app runs slow moving to workouts page' report when he next feels the app, especially on his personal/solo account
basis: Root cause matches exactly: the query was unconditionally fetched every load, up to 100 rows with a nested exercise join, against a coach_id account with a large historical orphan-template backlog -- worst-case precisely on the account type (personal/solo) most likely to have triggered the original report

claim: The new missed-check-to-test skill will actually fire at least once in the next 5 sessions -- a bug's root cause will match its trigger shape ('checked A, not the closely-related B') and produce a new Playwright test in the same commit as the fix, not just get created and forgotten
basis: Created directly from two real same-session instances of this exact pattern (the empty-phase accordion bug, the RLS embed-chain miss) and registered in hello-claude's standing behaviours -- but a freshly-created skill's actual future use rate is unproven until observed

claim: The client_programs/programs/program_phases/program_phase_workouts RLS fix will hold with no further gaps discovered in this specific embed chain -- the 4 policies applied are the complete set needed for a real client to see their assigned program end-to-end
basis: Verified end-to-end via a fresh Playwright fixture querying the exact nested-embed shape both app-dashboard.js and app-workouts.js actually use, asserting every level (programs, program_phases, program_phase_workouts) resolves with real data -- not just an isolated table-level check, which is exactly the verification gap that caused the first incomplete fix

claim: The program-picker fix (excluding program-owned templates from the reuse pool, relabeling the inline option) will fully resolve Jake's clutter complaint -- he will not ask for the originally-recommended tap-row live-filter UI redesign within the next month
basis: The actual complaint ("impossible for a PT to know which workout is which") was about pool size and duplicate ambiguity, both now fixed at the data level; the search-input UX itself is unchanged from the plain <select>, which was the OTHER half of the original 2026-07-08 recommendation. If list size stays large for other reasons (e.g. many genuinely-standalone templates), the UI complaint could resurface independently of today's fix.

claim: Jake will not ask for a retroactive reclassification pass on the exercises left merged by the is_personal fix -- the forward-only fix will be sufficient for him
basis: He explicitly said 'leave both lists as they are, but clean up the bug so this data doesn't bleed again' when given the choice -- a direct, unambiguous statement of preference, not a default he might reconsider.

claim: The workout_templates RLS tightening (adding `client_id is null` to the client-read policy) will not break any real client flow — no report of a missing programme, dashboard hero, or calendar session in the next month
basis: Traced every client-facing read before writing the SQL: clones (client_id set) resolve via the separate client_read_own_templates policy; masters (client_id null) and standalone professional templates stay permitted. Wrote a dedicated over-tightening guard test asserting the dashboard/calendar master-template embed chain still resolves, and it passes. Full suite 119 green post-change, including every client-facing spec.

claim: The decision NOT to enforce is_personal at the RLS layer will hold — it will not need revisiting, and no real exposure will arise from a client being able to read the coach's personal template/exercise names via a direct API call
basis: is_personal is a display split, not a tenancy boundary; the genuine boundary (coach_id + client_id) is now correctly enforced. The residual exposure is the coach's own training data, requires deliberate devtools work, and enforcing it at RLS would break the client dashboard/calendar master-template embed for any Personal-built programme assigned to a client.

claim: Jake will use the new Personal > Library page to build at least one genuinely reusable template (assigned to more than one day slot or programme), rather than continuing to use the inline '+ Create new workout (this day only)' flow
basis: This was the exact capability he identified as missing and the reason he raised it ('This is where I need to create workouts that can be put into programs, correct?'). But habit is sticky and the inline flow still works for one-offs; he may simply keep using what he knows.

claim: Jake will NOT ask to backfill/reclassify the 1537 pre-existing workout_templates rows that defaulted to is_personal = false — the open to-do will be dropped rather than actioned
basis: Matches his standing fix-forward-not-retroactive rule (feedback_fix_forward_not_retroactive, and his explicit call on the identical exercises.is_personal migration on 2026-07-10: 'leave both lists as they are, but clean up the bug so this data doesn't bleed again'). Medium not high because this time the misfiled rows are his OWN personal programme's templates showing in his PT list, which he may find more irritating in daily use than the exercises case.

claim: No further unguarded workout_templates delete path will be found — _deleteOwnedUnreferencedTemplates is now the single shared helper for both deletePhaseWeek and _cleanupPhaseWeeksBeyond, and deleteProgram has its own equivalent check
basis: Grepped every db.from('workout_templates').delete() call after the fix. Medium not high because this exact class has now been found TWICE in unrelated functions (deleteProgram 2026-07-10, _cleanupPhaseWeeksBeyond 2026-07-11) and each time it was believed complete afterwards.

claim: The tap-row workout picker will not need a follow-up fix for the 'which of my three Upper Body workouts is this' problem — name + description + exercise preview is enough to disambiguate in real use
basis: It replaces a native <select> whose <option> could only hold plain text, which was the actual root cause. But Jake has not used it on real data yet, and if his workouts share both a name AND similar first-3 exercises, the rows could still look alike.

claim: Jake will hit the orphaned-week-clone bug again (periodization clones surviving a program delete as fake 'standalone' templates cluttering his picker) before it is fixed, because it is currently only logged as a decision-needed item
basis: It already caused the 2026-07-10 picker-clutter report he raised himself, and it silently poisoned the E2E account this session. The mechanism (FK SET NULL on phase cascade) fires on every program deletion, and he deletes throwaway programs regularly.

claim: Jake will use 'Copy workouts to Library' on Hybrid Weapon Experiment as his first real action next session, rather than starting with the Part B features
basis: It is the thing he explicitly asked for ('so that I dont have to retype them all out'), it is one click, and his pattern is to try the thing he asked for immediately. Medium because he also tends to open a session by asking what is next rather than acting.

claim: The new-coach empty-app problem (0 exercises on signup) will be confirmed as the single biggest beta blocker -- if a real beta PT is onboarded before a default exercise library ships, they will stall or churn during their first session
basis: Verified in code: signUp creates the auth user, handle_new_user creates only the profiles row. No starter data of any kind. You cannot build a workout without exercises, and the only route is typing each one by hand. Jake's own account has 200+ exercises accumulated over months, so he has never seen the empty state.

claim: Jake will end up wanting error monitoring before beta, despite deprioritising it on 2026-07-11 -- the first time a beta user reports something vague ('it broke') with no way to see the actual error will change his mind
basis: He deprioritised it explicitly when offered. But his whole working pattern is to turn a failure into a permanent system, and a beta with no crash reporting means the failures arrive as anecdotes rather than stack traces. Medium, not high, because a small beta with users he knows personally may be debuggable by just asking them.

claim: The no-auto-fill change to the runner table will STICK -- Jake will not ask for pre-fill / 1-tap repeat back after using it in real sessions
basis: He reasoned about it himself ('should only be ghost text'), and the underlying argument is sound: a pre-filled value is indistinguishable from one you confirmed. But it costs him taps on every set of every workout, and tap-count under fatigue is exactly the kind of friction that changes minds in a gym. This is a genuine accuracy-vs-speed trade and he has only reasoned about it, not yet lived with it.

claim: The behavioural RLS audit harness will find at least one more real gap (unexpected-ALLOW or unexpected-DENY) that no one has flagged
basis: Three consecutive sessions each found a real RLS gap by accident while building something unrelated, and cross-COACH isolation has never been tested at all -- there is no second-coach account. A systematic sweep of a surface that yields a gap every time it is glanced at incidentally is very unlikely to come back clean.
result: TRUE, same session, and larger than predicted. Extended to Storage, the audit found a LIVE cross-tenant leak: `progress-photos` had 3 storage.objects policies scoped by bucket_id alone, so any authenticated coach could read AND delete any client's photos. Reproduced live (PT2 downloaded a real 1.79MB photo). An unexpected-ALLOW nobody had flagged. Also surfaced the Personal-Bests-never-displayed bug (broken query, not RLS) and confirmed the s23/s24 DENY class via Probe C. The prediction that a systematically-swept surface would not come back clean held immediately.

claim: A real coach signing up for beta will be unable to build their first workout without hand-typing every exercise, unless the empty-new-coach blocker is fixed first (default exercise library on signup)
basis: Verified in code: handle_new_user creates only the profiles row; signUp adds no exercises/templates/programs. You cannot build a workout without exercises. Jake has never hit this because his account has 200+ exercises from months of use. Beta is 31 July. This is the highest beta risk found and is now top of the to-do list but not yet started.

claim: The workout_logs fixture erosion (13 down to 4 across suite runs) is caused by a test deleting logs by client_id (or via a program cascade) without recreating them -- root cause will be in runner.spec.js or client-workout.spec.js, not the seed
basis: The seed is idempotent and skips when sessions exist, so it cannot be shrinking the count. Something in the suite is deleting the E2E client's real workout_logs and not restoring them. Same class as the 2026-07-10 deleteProgram data-loss (a test destroying fixture rows it did not own). The count only moves during full runs.

claim: The new-coach starter-content seed will work correctly for a REAL new signup (a genuine new coach lands on a populated dashboard with the 40 exercises + sample workout + sample program), first time, no manual fix needed
basis: The seed function + the loadUserInfo wiring are both proven via PT2 (a coach who owns nothing): 40 exercises, a 6-exercise linked sample workout, a Mon/Thu program, flag flip, idempotency, and a partial-failure resume all pass. handle_new_user is confirmed to give a self-signup role='coach'. The only thing untested is a real email signup end-to-end (Playwright can't drive that). Every component is verified; the integration risk is low.

claim: The 3 program-workflow fixes (stale-view-on-assign, surgical propagation, edit-reaches-calendar) will STICK — Jake will not re-report any of the three from real use
basis: All three were root-caused against the actual code and fixed with tests, and the multi-agent review caught 2 further real bugs in the first cut before push. But these are exactly the flows Jake exercises constantly in real coaching, and the propagation subsystem is where this project's data-loss bugs keep appearing — real use has surfaced things tests didn't, twice before.

claim: The week-tabs redesign (Programs builder + client Workouts page) will hold on Jake's own account with his real programs, no correction needed
basis: Playwright + screenshot verified on both surfaces incl. 390px no-scroll, but UI-perception issues have slipped past automated checks before (les-016)

claim: Jake will not ask to revert the restored per-workout Save-to-Library button (Option 1 over bulk-only was right)
basis: He chose Option 1 after I laid out the cherry-pick scenario; but nice-to-have features have died on contact with real use before (plate calculator)

claim: The 6-value metric_type model + typed workout_log_sets columns (avg_hr/max_hr/height_cm/side) will carry through sub-projects ②d/③/④ without needing a schema revision
basis: Designed capture before display so the shapes are driven by real logging needs, and ②c already exercises all 5 fast-table shapes end-to-end; but ③'s charts are the first real consumer and could surface a missing field

claim: When Jake logs a real unilateral/timed/jump exercise in the new fast table, he will accept the fast-table UX and not ask to bring back the step-by-step wizard for them
basis: Jake explicitly chose 'keep the Hevy-style, not the wizard' and the render was screenshot-verified at 390px; but UI-perception issues have slipped past automated checks before (les-016) and nice-to-haves have died on real gym use (plate calculator)
